author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
Even Evils, Through God’s Mercy, are of Use.
title: Even Evils, Through God’s Mercy, are of Use.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He asserts that “no evil is the cause of anything good;” as if punishment, forsooth, were good, although thereby many have been reformed.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: There are, then, evils which are of use by the wondrous mercy of God.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Did that man experience some good thing, when he said, “Thou didst hide Thy face from me, and I was troubled?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Certainly not; and yet this very trouble was to him in a certain manner a remedy against his pride.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For he had said in his prosperity, “I shall never be moved;” and so was ascribing to himself what he was receiving from the Lord.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “For what had he that he did not receive?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: It had, therefore, become necessary to show him whence he had received, that he might receive in humility what he had lost in pride.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Accordingly, he says, “In Thy good pleasure, O Lord, Thou didst add strength to my beauty.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: In this abundance of mine I once used to say, “I shall not be moved;” whereas it all came from Thee, not from myself.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Then at last Thou didst turn away Thy face from me, and I became troubled.
